exemplar.title: To what extent does social media usage impact depression amongst young adults?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does social media usage impact depression amongst young adults?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Accurate and effective communication of the topic with a clear and appropriate purpose
Consistent return to research question throughout major sections
Explicit listing of five peerâreviewed core studies showing informed selection
weaknesses: Research question is clear but overly broad in scope
No explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria or documented search strategy
Minor digressions (EEG theory) that slightly blur the focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (research question, methodology) sit in the 3â4 band and one strand (topic communication) in the 5â6 band. Overall performance is above a solid 4 yet shy of a full 6; a mark of 5 reflects the clear purpose and informed method choice tempered by breadth and transparency issues.

strengths: Sound grasp of key constructs (FOMO, BDI-II, frontal alpha asymmetry) and experimental design terminology
Correct reporting of p-values and detailed longitudinal design description
weaknesses: Reliance on non-peer-reviewed sources (Wikipedia, Forbes, verywellmind.com) for key definitions and statistics
Minor citation errors and inconsistent terminology usage
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands (subject knowledge/application and use of terminology) align with the 3â4 band: good understanding and mostly accurate application, but undermined by non-academic sources and occasional precision errors. A topâofâband mark of 4 is therefore appropriate.

strengths: Coherent synthesis of findings and comparison of methodologies with a balanced conclusion
Identification of moderating variables (e.g., cyberbullying, FOMO) and methodological strengths/weaknesses
Accurate highlighting of bidirectional ambiguity in correlational designs
weaknesses: Analysis sometimes remains descriptive with unsupported assertions
Missing effect sizes (RÂ²) and confidence intervals when reporting correlations
No discussion of ethical considerations (informed consent, participant distress)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of critical thinking fall squarely in the mid 4â6 band. The student demonstrates solid research relevance, clear analysis, and good evaluation. Awarding the top of this band (6) recognizes the strength of synthesis and evaluation while acknowledging remaining depth and ethical discussion gaps.

strengths: Clearly appropriate overall structure (title page, contents, headings, bibliography)
Required word count met, alphabetized bibliography, table of contents titled correctly
weaknesses: Inconsistent or duplicated section headings and erratic citation formatting
Broken URLs and missing page numbers in the table of contents
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Performance meets the 3â4 band for presentation: conventions are largely respected and structure is sound. Assigning the top mark of 4 reflects solid adherence to formatting requirements despite minor layout and navigation issues.

strandSelections.reasoning: Subject-specific terms such as "bidirectional ambiguity", "self-selected sampling", "internal validity" and "likert scale" are used mostly correctly and in appropriate contexts.  Consistency falters where the student inserts non-standard abbreviations or typing errors ("Thid", "yerywellmind").  Overall usage is accurate enough for the upper 3-4 band but not uniformly precise to justify the top band.

The work includes the required word count, remains under 4000 words and displays a clear table of contents.  Hyperlinks, superscripts and some broken URLs detract from professional appearance, and page numbering is unclear from the extract, but overall layout conventions are largely respected, justifying a low 3-4 band award.

The student accurately outlines key psychological constructs (FOMO, BDI-II, frontal alpha asymmetry) and demonstrates a sound grasp of experimental design terminology (IV, DV, ecological validity, demand characteristics).  Study details are generally correct, though a few citation errors and minor misunderstandings appear (e.g., equating BDI cut-offs with clinical diagnosis).  Application of knowledge to the research question is clear but occasionally superficial, so the work aligns with âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ knowledge.

The essay is based on secondary research; five core peer-reviewed studies are selected and summarised, supported by additional statistics and theory.  Source types (true experiment, field experiment, correlational studies, content analysis) show a reasonable range and there is some evidence of informed choice (e.g., inclusion of the only longitudinal field experiment available).  However, several sources are popular websites (Wikipedia, Forbes) and no explicit justification of inclusion criteria or search strategy is provided.  This places the work firmly in the âmostly completeâ rather than âfully completeâ category.

The title, abstract sentence and opening paragraph state both the broad topic (social-media use and depression) and the precise population (young adults).  Throughout the essay the student consistently returns to this focus and indicates why it is worth studying, so the purpose is clear to the reader.  Minor lapses in phrasing and some digressions about EEG theory do not seriously blur the communication of the topic.  Overall the work meets the descriptor for a clear and appropriate purpose, meriting a high-band award.

Five core empirical studies, each clearly relevant to the question, are examined alongside prevalence statistics and theoretical frameworks.  Although some sources (Wikipedia, popular media statistics) lower academic rigour, the majority are peer-reviewed and appropriate.  The selection provides adequate depth and breadth, placing quality and relevance at a solid mid-band (4-6) level.

The research question ("To what extent does social media usage impact depression amongst young adults?") is explicitly stated and is referred to at the start of most major sections, demonstrating coherence.  Nevertheless, the formulation is quite broad (all forms of social-media use, all manifestations of depression) and the student sometimes moves beyond the behavioural focus by discussing EEG asymmetry and psychosocial identity formation.  Hence the question is clear but only partially focused, fitting the 3-4 band.

The essay follows conventional EE structure with title page, contents, section headings, discussion and bibliography.  Citations appear in-text and the bibliography is alphabetised.  Nevertheless, some headings are duplicated or inconsistent, and citation formatting is erratic, so a full 4/4 is not warranted.  A score of 3 within the 3-4 band is appropriate.

The discussion section synthesises findings, compares methodologies and acknowledges moderating variables (cyberbullying, FOMO).  The argument is coherent and structured, leading to a cautiously balanced conclusion.  Critical evaluation is present but not consistently penetrating; for example, issues of cultural bias and operationalisation are noted but not explored in depth.  This aligns with a mid-band (5) performance for discussion and evaluation.

For each study the student identifies design features and strengths/limitations, linking them to how convincingly they address the RQ.  However, much of the commentary is descriptive (âwhat was done/what was foundâ) and evaluative points are sometimes asserted without deeper methodological justification.  The analysis is therefore clear and partly supported, consistent with the lower end of the 4-6 band.

annotations.comment: Strength: The title clearly states the topic and scope, providing a concise overview of the investigationâs focus.
Weakness: Research question is clear but overly broad, encompassing all forms of social media and depression; narrowing the scope would improve focus.
Strength: Word count is correctly formatted and remains under the 4000-word limit.
Weakness: Table of contents lists section titles without page numbers, making it difficult for the reader to navigate the document.
Strength: The table of contents is clearly titled and structured, conforming to presentation conventions.
Weakness: Reliance on non-peer-reviewed sources (Backlinko, Forbes) for prevalence statistics undermines the academic rigor of the introduction.
Suggestion: Complete the clinical definition of depression by specifying that symptoms must persist for at least two consecutive weeks.
Weakness: Wikipedia is used as the primary source for defining FOMO; replacing this with a peer-reviewed article would strengthen credibility.
Suggestion: Explicitly link Sternâs discussion of online authorship back to the research question to clarify its relevance to depression outcomes.
Strength: The methodology section lists five peer-reviewed studies chosen to address the research question, demonstrating informed selection.
Weakness: The selection of studies lacks an explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria or documented search strategy, reducing transparency.
Suggestion: Correct the typo âThidâ and ensure consistent citation formatting for the EEG protocol source.
Strength: The concept of frontal alpha asymmetry is clearly explained, showing solid understanding of its relevance to mood regulation.
Suggestion: Include effect sizes (e.g., RÂ²) when reporting correlations to quantify the strength of associations.
Strength: Results section correctly reports p-values for experimental and control conditions, enhancing result transparency.
Suggestion: Include confidence intervals alongside p-values to convey effect size and precision of the findings.
Weakness: Discussion of self-selected sampling bias does not explicitly connect how this might skew depression results in the experiment.
Suggestion: Consider stratified random sampling to improve generalizability and reduce self-selection bias.
Weakness: Reliance on verywellmind.com (typo in URL âyerywellmindâ) for BDI-II details indicates inconsistency; use the primary source instead.
Strength: Accurately highlights bidirectional ambiguity in correlational design, demonstrating critical awareness.
Suggestion: Clarify that BDI-II cut-offs represent symptom severity rather than a clinical diagnosis to prevent misinterpretation.
Strength: Detailed description of the longitudinal field experiment design by Hunt et al. shows strong methodological understanding.
Suggestion: Discuss attrition rates and their impact on validity to provide a fuller evaluation of the longitudinal design.
Weakness: Lacks discussion of ethical considerations such as informed consent and potential distress when exposing participants to depression memes.
Strength: Synthesizes findings from multiple correlational studies, highlighting consistent patterns across contexts.
Suggestion: Report intercoder reliability metrics for the manual annotation in the content analysis to address potential bias.
Strength: The discussion synthesizes methodological strengths and weaknesses across studies, demonstrating strong evaluative depth.
Suggestion: Consider integrating qualitative methods (e.g., interviews) to enrich contextual understanding of user experiences.
Weakness: Conclusion restates findings broadly without specific, actionable recommendations for future research.
Strength: Bibliography entries are alphabetized, which aids reader navigation and reflects good presentation practice.
Weakness: Citation formatting is inconsistent, with a mix of full URLs and plain text; a uniform APA or MLA style should be adopted.
